issues.title: Rust PyO3 extension build fails (maturin develop)

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Repro
From repo root:
- `cd rust`
- `/opt/anaconda3/bin/conda run -n virtuus maturin develop --release`

## Result
Build fails with multiple compile errors in `rust/src/python.rs` (PyO3 0.21 API changes / borrow issues). Examples:
- E0282 type annotations needed (depythonize on `junction.as_ref(py)`)
- E0599 `borrow` not found for `&Py<PyList>` (should use bound/as_ref)
- E0596 cannot borrow `table` as mutable (missing `mut`)
- E0716 temporary value dropped while borrowed (`fk_value.to_string()` inline)
- E0502 mutable/immutable borrow conflict in `table.association_defs()` + `table.scan()`

## Impact
Blocks Rust backend import (`virtuus._rust`) and therefore Rust-side benchmarks via Python harness.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Progress (2026-02-23)
Implemented fixes in `rust/src/python.rs` to restore PyO3 build compatibility:
- fixed mutable borrows (`export`, `describe`, `validate`)
- resolved borrow conflicts in `validate` by cloning defs and scanning once
- avoided temporary borrow issues for FK strings
- updated PyDict `get_item` handling to account for PyResult
- fixed `scan` to collect pythonized results without type mismatch
- switched junction depythonize to `depythonize_bound` using `into_bound`
- updated hook iteration to use `bind`

`cargo check --features python` now passes (warnings only), and `maturin develop --release` succeeds. `virtuus._rust` import verified.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: Done: PyO3 build fixed; maturin develop --release succeeds and virtuus._rust imports. Next: close issue. Blockers: none.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Perf improvement options
- **Profile hot paths (Rust):** perf/flamegraph on GSI query + PK lookup; focus on clones/allocs in `table.rs` (Vec sorting, serde_json Value handling).
- **Index structures:** use `BTreeMap` per GSI partition to keep sorted order without per-query sort; consider `FxHashMap` for PK map to reduce hashing cost.
- **Serde overhead:** try typed structs or borrowing `Value`/`Cow` to avoid clones on hot paths.
- **I/O batching:** reuse buffers and `BufWriter` for export/persistence; reduce syscalls.
- **Parallel load:** rayon-based directory load + GSI build per partition to speed cold load.
- **Query caching:** optional per-GSI partition cache of last sorted slice for repeated scans.
- **Instrumentation:** add tracing spans around load/query to split time between sort vs deserialization.

Next: run perf on 100k corpus, prototype BTreeMap GSI, measure vs baseline.

comments.text:
## Experiment: Parallel file IO (Rayon) in `Table::load_from_dir`
Approach: gather JSON paths, read+parse in parallel, then sequentially `put` + manifest updates.

### Bench results (10k `full_database_cold_load`, 5 runs)
- Rust (parallel attempt): mean **2934.240 ms**, median 2869.528

- Python baseline: mean **3301.024 ms**, median 3126.695

issues.title: Rust performance investigation --status in_progress
issues.description:
## Goal
Make Rust cold-load meaningfully faster than Python for record loading; target up to ~10x if feasible.

## Context
Rust version currently performs similar to Python, which is unexpected. We need to leverage Rust for speed (e.g., parallel load) and empirically validate improvements.

## Approach
For each performance idea: implement, ensure specs pass, benchmark vs Rust baseline and Python, and keep only if gains justify complexity. If not, revert to baseline and record what was tried/learned.

## Tracking
Create one story per idea with separate notes; work may use Git worktrees for isolation.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: Context from user (2026-02-23): Surprised Rust perf matches Python. Expect up to ~10x faster for this task. Explore multiple Rust performance ideas (e.g., parallel loading), implement each, verify specs/tests, benchmark vs Rust baseline and Python. Keep/merge only if empirical gains justify added complexity; otherwise revert to baseline and note failed idea + learnings. Create a new story per idea and track notes separately. Git worktrees ok for isolation.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Conclusion (2026-02-23)
The benchmark harness is Python-based. When `VIRTUUS_BENCH_BACKEND=rust`, it tries to import `virtuus._rust`; if that import fails it silently falls back to the Python backend.

On this machine, the Rust PyO3 extension currently **does not build**, so `virtuus._rust` is not importable. As a result, attempts to run “Rust” benchmarks are actually exercising the Python backend. This explains why historical results show Rust and Python with equivalent performance.

Evidence:
- `maturin develop --release` fails with compile errors in `rust/src/python.rs` (PyO3 API/borrow issues) → `virtuus._rust` missing.
- Benchmark JSON does not record backend; Python harness defaults to Python when Rust import fails.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Progress (2026-02-23)
- Fixed PyO3 build issues in `rust/src/python.rs`; `maturin develop --release` now succeeds and `virtuus._rust` imports.
- Captured 10k yardstick baselines:
  - Python full_database_cold_load: 3354.440 ms
  - Rust full_database_cold_load: 2929.900 ms

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: Done: CI green and semantic-release published 0.2.1; PyO3 build fixed and 10k baselines recorded. Next: close epic. Blockers: none.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: Correction: reopened; this epic is still in progress. Next: continue Rust perf investigations. Blockers: none.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Rust-native benchmark update (2026-02-23)
Ran Rust directly via CLI (no Python harness). `cargo run --release --bin virtuus -- query` with schema loading all tables, 10k dataset. Mean load+query time: **2807.248 ms** (3 runs). Details logged on task virt-4a074a.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Experiment result: Parallel IO (Rayon)
Tried parallelizing `Table::load_from_dir` with Rayon (read+parse in parallel, sequential put). Benchmarks at 10k `full_database_cold_load` showed **no meaningful improvement** (mean ~2934 ms vs prior ~2930 ms). Changes were reverted and dependency removed.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Experiment result: byte reads + preallocation
Tried `fs::read` + `serde_json::from_slice` with pre-allocation in `Table::load_from_dir`. 10k `full_database_cold_load` showed no meaningful improvement (median ~2884 ms, mean worse due outlier). Reverted change.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Win: Load is now read-only
Changed `load_from_dir` to skip disk writes during load (Python + Rust), aligning with README (only `put`/`delete` write). 10k `full_database_cold_load` improved from ~2.9–3.3s to ~0.48s (mean Rust 478.758 ms, Python 492.872 ms). Keeping change.

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text: New experiment: parallel JSON parsing in Rust load_from_dir (Rayon). Bench (10k full_database_cold_load): baseline mean 478.758 ms -> new mean 388.146 ms (~19% faster). CI passed; change committed in repo.

issues.title: Skip disk writes during load (Python + Rust)

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
Linked story: virt-7ecdb6
Goal: make `load_from_dir` read-only by bypassing disk writes during load in both Python and Rust while preserving indexing + hooks.
Benchmark gate: compare 10k `full_database_cold_load` vs Rust baseline (2929.900 ms) and Python baseline (3354.440 ms).

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
## Results: skip disk writes during load
Change: `load_from_dir` now inserts into memory + indexes + hooks without rewriting files (Python + Rust).

### Bench results (10k `full_database_cold_load`, 5 runs)
- Rust runs: [449.211, 427.741, 448.473, 576.032, 492.336]
- mean **478.758 ms**, median **449.211 ms**
- Python runs: [479.577, 464.932, 478.768, 487.738, 553.343]
- mean **492.872 ms**, median **479.577 ms**

### Decision
Significant improvement (~6x faster vs prior ~2.9–3.3s). Keep change.
